title: A Systematic Literature Review of Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Techniques, Metrics, and Challenges
one_line_takeaway: RAG is moving toward modular, provenance-aware, uncertainty-controlled architectures, which directly supports a semantic COP that must brief operators from traceable local evidence under degraded connectivity.
d4d_relevance: The paper supports the T3 semantic COP argument that LLM-generated maritime briefs should be grounded in retrieved mission data, doctrine, sensor reports, and prior COP state rather than model memory alone. Its emphasis on policy-driven retrieval, uncertainty loops, provenance, latency/cost tradeoffs, and security maps well to denied-network maritime operations where bandwidth, trust, stale data, and adversarial information are central risks.
input_data: 128 empirical RAG-related studies from 2020 to May 2025
Search results from ACM Digital Library, IEEE Xplore, Scopus, ScienceDirect, and DBLP
Knowledge-intensive QA, open-domain QA, software engineering, and medical RAG task studies
Evaluation evidence covering retrieval quality, answer quality, human judgment, LLM-as-judge, efficiency, and safety
methods_or_architecture: Descriptive synthesis organized by research questions rather than meta-analysis
Mapping of RAG architectures from early DPR/seq2seq systems to modular policy-driven systems
Comparison of retrieval diagnostics, generation metrics, human evaluation, and emerging LLM-as-judge protocols
Identification of architecture patterns using hybrid retrieval, structured retrieval, memory, uncertainty gating, and multimodal inputs
outputs_metrics: Included-study counts by domain, including knowledge-intensive tasks, open-domain QA, software engineering, and medical domains
Traditional answer metrics such as exact match and F1
Retrieval metrics such as Recall@k and MRR@k
Human assessment and LLM-as-judge evaluation patterns
Recommended benchmark dimensions covering quality, cost, latency, uncertainty, provenance, and safety
prototype_hooks: Build the COP assistant as a local-first RAG pipeline over cached tracks, reports, orders, intelligence notes, nautical references, and doctrine
Use hybrid retrieval combining keyword, vector, and structured/graph search over vessels, locations, events, confidence, and timestamps
Attach every generated COP statement to source snippets, sensor reports, or structured records with provenance and freshness metadata
Add uncertainty-triggered behavior: retrieve more evidence, ask for confirmation, defer judgment, or flag low-confidence claims
Implement bandwidth-aware retrieval and synchronization policies for disconnected or intermittently connected maritime environments
Evaluate the prototype with both answer usefulness and operational constraints: latency, token budget, bandwidth, stale-data handling, citation accuracy, and safety
Add adversarial-data tests for retrieval poisoning, sensitive-data leakage, jailbreak attempts, and misleading maritime reports
pitch_evidence: The review synthesizes 128 studies, giving broad support for RAG as a serious architectural pattern rather than a one-off demo technique
The paper reports a field-level shift toward modular, policy-driven RAG, matching the proposed COP architecture rather than simple chatbot retrieval
It highlights provenance and traceable evidence as requirements for dependable systems, directly supporting operator trust in a maritime COP
It identifies cost, latency, and security as growing evaluation concerns, which are core to denied-network deployments
It supports combining retrieval quality, generation quality, human judgment, and safety metrics in a prototype evaluation plan
limitations_risks: Only the abstract/excerpt was available here, so details from the full review may be missing
The review is broad and not maritime-specific; maritime COP adaptation requires domain-specific retrieval schemas and test data
No meta-analysis was performed, so evidence is synthesized descriptively rather than as pooled effect sizes
The included literature may be affected by citation lag, English-only filtering, and database coverage limits
RAG itself introduces risks from poisoned sources, stale caches, leakage of sensitive operational data, and overconfident summaries
